metadata.title: Integrated multi-omics analysis reveals liver metabolic reprogramming by fish iridovirus and antiviral function of alpha-linolenic acid
metadata.abstract:
Iridovirus poses a substantial threat to global aquaculture due to its high mortality rate; however, the molecular mechanisms  underpinning  its  pathogenesis  are  not  well elucidated.  Here,  a  multi-omics  approach  was  applied  to groupers infected with Singapore grouper iridovirus (SGIV), focusing on the roles of key metabolites. Results showed that SGIV induced obvious histopathological damage and changes in metabolic enzymes within the liver. Furthermore, SGIV significantly reduced the contents of lipid droplets, triglycerides, cholesterol, and lipoproteins. Metabolomic analysis indicated that the altered metabolites were enriched in 19 pathways, with a notable down-regulation of lipid metabolites such as glycerophosphates and alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), consistent with disturbed lipid homeostasis in the liver. Integration of transcriptomic and metabolomic data revealed  that  the  top  enriched  pathways  were  related  to cell growth and death and nucleotide, carbohydrate, amino acid, and lipid metabolism, supporting the conclusion that SGIV infection induced liver metabolic reprogramming. Further  integrative  transcriptomic  and  proteomic  analysis indicated  that  SGIV  infection  activated  crucial  molecular events in a phagosome-immune depression-metabolism dysregulation-necrosis signaling cascade. Of note, integrative multi-omics analysis demonstrated the consumption of ALA and linoleic acid (LA) metabolites, and the accumulation of L-glutamic acid (GA), accompanied by alterations in immune, inflammation, and cell death-related genes. metadata.introduction:
Iridoviruses are important emerging pathogens that impact ectothermic vertebrates (including fish, frogs, newts, and reptiles) and invertebrates (including insects and crustaceans) worldwide.  These  viruses  have  attracted  increasing  attention due to their role in the decline of amphibian populations and the substantial economic losses they cause in the global aquaculture  industry  (Chinchar  et al.,  2017; Qin  et al.,  2023; Zhao  et al.,  2023).  According  to  the  International  Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses (https://ictv.global/taxonomy), iridoviruses are currently divided into seven genera, including _Iridovirus_ , _Megalocytivirus_ , _Ranavirus_ , _Lymphocystivirus_ , _Chloriridovirus_ , _Decapodiridovirus_ , and _Daphniairidovirus_ (Chen et al., 2019; Qin et al., 2023). As a typical agent of fish iridovirus, the Singapore grouper iridovirus (SGIV), belonging to  the  genus _Ranavirus_ ,  was  initially  isolated  from  diseased groupers  ( _Epinephelus  tauvina_ )  and  has  caused  significant mortality  and  economic  losses  in  grouper  aquaculture  (Qin 

Received: 26 January 2024; Accepted: 11 March 2024; Online: 12 March 2024

*Corresponding authors, E-mail: qinqw@scau.edu.cn; huangyh@scau. edu.cn; huangxh@scau.edu.cn 

et al., 2003). Subsequent studies have progressively elucidated the viral morphogenesis process and potential mechanisms  of  viral  entry,  assembly,  transport,  and  cellular fate following SGIV infection (Huang et al., 2011a; Wang et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2023). Moreover, annotations of the viral genome, transcriptome, and proteome have enabled investigations into the function of crucial genes in viral replication and immune evasion (Chen et al., 2006; Teng et al., 2008; Zhou et al., 2011). 

In addition to advancements in understanding virus functional genes, single “omics” based approaches have been applied  to  study  the  potential  mechanisms  underlying  host responses to SGIV interaction. Transcriptomic analysis of the spleen  of _Epinephelus  coioides_ revealed  that  SGIV  infection alters genes associated with various signaling pathways, including mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), chemokine, toll-like receptor, and retinoic acid-inducible gene1  (RIG-I)  (Huang  et al.,  2011b).  Transcriptomic  analysis  of grouper spleen (GS) cells indicated that SGIV infection disrupts poly (I:C) stimulation-induced interferon (IFN) immune and inflammatory signaling pathways (Wang et al., 2023), while  lipidomic  analysis  showed  that  SGIV  infection  disturbs glycerophospholipid  (GP)  homeostasis  and  alters  fatty  acyl (FA) content (Ni et al., 2021). These omics data have facilitated the identification of many genes involved in immune response, cell apoptosis, cell autophagy, and metabolism, clarifying their roles in SGIV infection (Huang et al., 2015; Li et al.,  2019; Zhang  et al.,  2020, 2021; Zheng  et al.,  2022). However, no studies have yet illustrated the molecular events involved  in  SGIV  infection  at  the  systemic  level  or  identified viable antiviral targets for the prevention and control of SGIV. 

Due to their comprehensive metrics, multi-omics technologies offer extensive insights into the molecular events underlying  the  pathogenesis  of  different  diseases  (Eckhardt et al., 2020; Stukalov et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). Integrated  transcriptomic  and  proteomic  analyses  of  bovine viral diarrhea virus (BVDV)-infected cells have identified multiple cellular signaling pathways, including nucleotide oligomerization domain (NOD)-like receptor, toll-like receptor, nuclear factor (NF)-κB, MAPK, lipid metabolism, and apoptosis signaling pathways, involved in viral replication and pathogenesis  (Ma  et al.,  2022).  Similarly,  combined  analysis of metabolomes, proteomes, and transcriptomes in hepatitis C virus (HCV)-infected cells showed an increase in glucose metabolism and activation of the signal transducer and activator of transcription 3 (STAT3) signaling pathway, thereby reducing peroxisome function (Lupberger et al., 2019). In recent years, integrated omics analyses have also proven effective in the development of novel diagnostics and therapeutic drugs against viral diseases (Bi et al., 2022; Eisfeld et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2024). For example, the metabolite  linoleic  acid  (LA)  has  been  found  to  inhibit  the replication and release of porcine epidemic diarrhea virus (PEDV) from Vero-E6 cells by inhibiting protein kinase B phosphorylation  (Yang  et al.,  2023),  and  to  suppress  white spot syndrome virus (WSSV) replication by enhancing the expression of antimicrobial peptides and IFN-like gene _Vago5_ via the extracellular signal-regulated kinase (ERK)-NF-κB signaling pathway (Li et al., 2021). Additionally, alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), another ω-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid, has been confirmed as a broad-spectrum antiviral agent capable of inhibiting dengue virus (DENV), herpes simplex virus 1 (HSV1), and severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 

(SARS-CoV-2) replication (Feng et al., 2023). In addition, treatments  with  glutamic  acid  (GA)  have  been  reported  to promote  DENV-2  infection  and  dissemination  in  mosquitoes (Zhu et al., 2017). Thus, the identification and characterization of  key  metabolites  involved  in  viral  replication  through  multiomics analyses are critical for providing novel approaches for the prevention and control of viral diseases. 

In the present study, we investigated changes in histopathology and lipid content in the livers of SGIV-infected groupers. Subsequently, we generated transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolomic profiles from the livers of infected groupers and performed integrated multi-omics analysis of crucial molecular events at the gene-, protein-, and metabolite levels associated with SGIV infection in the liver. Among the screened metabolites, we investigated the effects of LA, ALA, and GA during SGIV infection _in vitro_ through the exogenous application of these metabolites. Overall, this study provides a systematic understanding of the molecular events involved in fish iridovirus pathogenesis and contributes to the development of novel approaches for the prevention and control of fish viruses.

evidence.original_text: Iridovirus poses a substantial threat to global aquaculture due to its high mortality rate; however, the molecular mechanisms underpinning its pathogenesis are not well elucidated. Here, a multi-omics approach was applied to groupers infected with Singapore grouper iridovirus (SGIV), focusing on the roles of key metabolites. Results showed that SGIV induced obvious histopathological damage and changes in metabolic enzymes within the liver. Furthermore, SGIV significantly reduced the contents of lipid droplets, triglycerides, cholesterol, and lipoproteins. Metabolomic analysis indicated that the altered metabolites were enriched in 19 pathways, with a notable down-regulation of lipid metabolites such as glycerophosphates and alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), consistent with disturbed lipid homeostasis in the liver. Integration of transcriptomic and metabolomic data revealed that the top enriched pathways were related to cell growth and death and nucleotide, carbohydrate, amino acid, and lipid metabolism, supporting the conclusion that SGIV infection induced liver metabolic reprogramming. Further integrative transcriptomic and proteomic analysis indicated that SGIV infection activated crucial molecular events in a phagosome-immune depression-metabolism dysregulation-necrosis signaling cascade. Of note, integrative multi-omics analysis demonstrated the consumption of ALA and linoleic acid (LA) metabolites, and the accumulation of L-glutamic acid (GA), accompanied by alterations in immune, inflammation, and cell death-related genes. Further experimental data showed that ALA, but not GA, suppressed SGIV replication by activating antioxidant and anti-inflammatory responses in the host. Collectively, these findings provide a comprehensive resource for understanding host response dynamics during fish iridovirus infection and highlight the antiviral potential of ALA in the prevention and treatment of iridoviral diseases.

Firstly, 30 mg of liver tissue was accurately weighed, ground, and ultrasound-extracted with 400 μL of methanol (80%) containing 20 μL of L-2chlorophenylalanine (0.3 mg/mL, methanol configuration). After centrifugation at 13 000 r/min at 4°C for 10 min, 300 μL of the supernatant was isolated and diluted with 200 μL of methanol (80%). The supernatant was then vortexed, ultrasound-extracted, and centrifuged at 13 000 r/min at 4°C for 10 min. Finally, 150 μL of supernatant was collected for LC-MS analysis. Quality control (QC) samples were obtained from each sample. The ACQUITY UHPLC system (Waters Corporation, USA) coupled with the AB SCIEX Triple TOF 5600 System (AB SCIEX, USA) was used for metabolic profiling in both ESI positive and negative ion modes. All 16 samples (n=8 for MOCK and SGIV groups) were randomly injected. The raw data files were processed using Progenesis QI v.2.3, and compounds were identified using the EMDB database. Principal component analysis (PCA) and partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) were performed. Variable importance in projection (VIP) was conducted using orthogonal PLS-DA (OPLS-DA) analysis. Univariate analysis (Student’s t-test) was applied to calculate statistical significance (P-value). Metabolites with VIP>1 and P<0.05 were identified as differentially expressed metabolites (DEMs), and the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database was used to link these metabolites to metabolic pathways.

Total RNA was extracted from three liver samples in the SGIV and MOCK groups, respectively, using a mirVana™ miRNA ISOlation Kit (Invitrogen, USA), according to the manufacturer’s instructions. RNA concentration and purity were measured using a NanoDrop 2000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA), while RNA integrity was detected using an Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer (Agilent Technologies, USA) and 1% agarose gel electrophoresis. The extracted RNA (OD260/280≥1.9, OD260/230≥1.9, and RIN≥7) was used for transcriptomic analysis and quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (qPCR), which was performed to validate gene expression levels. Eukaryotic mRNA was enriched by magnetic beads with Oligo(dT) and broken into short fragments via the addition of interrupting reagents. Double-strand cDNA was then synthesized, purified, end-modified with A-tail, and connected with sequencing splices. Libraries were constructed using a TruSeq Stranded mRNA LT Sample Prep Kit (Illumina, USA) and sequenced on the Illumina sequencing platform (HiSeqTM 2500, USA). Transcriptomic sequencing and analysis were conducted by OE Biotech (China). At least 7 Gb of raw bases were generated per sample, with a GC ratio exceeding 48% and percentage of Q30 bases of 94%, indicating that the transcriptomic data were qualified for subsequent analysis (Supplementary Table S1). The raw data were processed using Trimmomatic, and clean reads were assembled using Trinity v.2.4. Unigene functions were annotated using the NCBI nonredundant (NR), SwissProt, and Clusters of Orthologous Groups for Eukaryotic Complete Genomes (KOG) databases using BLASTX (Altschul et al., 1990). The unigenes were mapped based on Gene Ontology (GO) and KEGG analysis according to SwissProt ID. The eXpress program (Roberts & Pachter, 2013) was used to obtain the number of unigene reads per sample, and the DESeq (2012) R package was used to standardize the data and calculate the P-value and foldchange of differences (Anders & Huber, 2013), with P<0.05 and |log2[Fold-change (FC)]|≥1 set as the thresholds of differentially expressed genes (DEGs). PCA was performed based on the expression of unigenes in each sample.

The proteins from three liver samples in the MOCK and SGIV groups were extracted by phenol extraction solution with phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride. evidence.original_text: Histopathological changes in the liver were determined by oil red O and hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. In brief, the liver tissues were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde overnight. Samples for H&E staining were dehydrated with the 70%–100% gradient of ethanol and embedded in paraffin, then sliced using a slicing machine (LEICA 819, Shanghai Leica Instruments, China). Samples for oil red O staining were dehydrated with 15% and 30% sucrose solution at 4°C and embedded with optimal cutting temperature compound agent, then sliced using a freezing microtome (CRYOSTAR NX50, Thermo Scientific, USA). The samples were dyed with oil red O and H&E, respectively, then sealed with neutral resins and observed under a Nikon Eclipse E100 microscope (Japan). Three microscope fields for each sample were randomly examined to calculate the ratio of the red pixel area to total pixel area of the oil red O-stained liver sections. Pixels were determined using ImageJ (NIH, USA).

GS cells were treated with different concentrations of metabolites (diluted in cell culture medium) for 48 h to detect cell viability using a CCK8 Assay Kit (BioScience, China) according to the manufacturer’s protocols. Untreated cells were considered as a positive control, while wells without GS cells but containing culture medium were used as blank controls. Results were expressed as the percentage cell viability, determined as the ratio of the optical density in the experimental wells to the optical density in the control wells. Data are represented as the mean±standard deviation (SD) of six replicates.

Total RNA was isolated using a Cell Total RNA Isolation Kit (Foregene, China) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. RNA size distribution and concentration were analyzed using RNA 6000 Nano LabChips (Agilent Technologies, USA) and processed using an Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer with the total RNA electrophoresis program. Reverse transcription was performed using a ReverTra AceTM qPCR RT Kit (TOYOBO, Japan) according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

qPCR was performed using a CFX384 Touch Real-Time PCR Detection System (Bio-Rad, USA). SYBR Green reactions were performed using 2×Polarsignal qPCR mix (with low ROX) (MIKX, China) with the following program: denaturation at 95°C for 10 min; 40 cycles of amplification at 95°C for 10 s, 60°C for 10 s, and 72°C for 30 s; final elongation at 72°C for 7 min; and final dissociation at 95°C for 15 s, 60°C for 15 s, and 95°C for 15 s. The 10 μL qPCR reaction mixture included 5 μL of 2×Polarsignal qPCR mix, 0.4 μL of forward and reverse primers (10 μmol/L) each, 1 μL of cDNA (5 ng/mL), and 3.2 μL of RNase H2O. Each PCR assay was performed three times independently in triplicate. 18S rRNA or β-actin was used as a reference. The primers were designed according to published sequences (Supplementary Table S2). To calculate gene expression, the comparative cycle threshold (CT) (2−ΔΔCT) method was adopted.

entries.name: RNA extraction, reverse transcription, and qPCR analysis

evidence.original_text: Cell samples from different groups were lysed with ice-cold Pierce RIPA lysis buffer (Thermo Scientific, USA). After centrifugation at 12 000 r/min 4°C for 5 min, the extracted protein was quantified using the Bradford assay. To the total protein, 5×SDS protein loading buffer (Epizyme Biotech, China) was added and then denatured by boiling for 10 min. Equal amounts of protein were separated by 10% SDS-PAGE and blotted onto 0.22 μm polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membranes (Millipore, USA). Tris HCl solution with 0.1% Tween-20 and defatted milk (5%) was used to block the membrane at 25°C for 2 h. The membranes were then incubated with primary antibodies, including anti-MCP, antiVP19, anti-VP118, anti-Nrf2, and anti-β-tubulin, at 4°C overnight. After washing with PBS containing 0.5% Tween 20 (PBST), the membranes were incubated with anti-mouse or anti-rabbit secondary horseradish peroxidase (HRP)conjugated antibodies (Abcam, USA) for 1 h. Lastly, the target protein was visualized using ECL reagent (Vazyme, China) and quantified using ImageJ.

Viral titers in GS cells were assessed to determine the effects of GA, LA, and ALA on SGIV production. In brief, the GS cells were pretreated with GA, LA, and ALA or mock treated for 12 h, then infected with 10 μL of SGIV (108 TCID50/mL) and collected at 24 h post-infection (hpi). Subsequently, viral titers of cell lysates were evaluated using the 50% tissue culture infectious dose (TCID50) assay. Cytopathic effects (CPEs) were observed under a light microscope (Leica, Germany) daily, and each sample was measured in triplicate.

The GS cells were seeded into 24-well plates at 60%–80% confluence overnight, then transfected with 100 ng of pRLSV40 reporter plasmid, 600 ng of pGL3-antioxidant response element (ARE)-Luc reporter (Clontech, USA), or pGL3-NF-κBLuc reporter (Clontech, USA) using Lipofectamine 2000. After 6 h, the cells were treated with ALA for 12 h, then infected with 10 μL of SGIV (108 TCID50/mL). The cells were harvested at 24 hpi and lysed. Luciferase assays were then carried out using the Dual-Luciferase Reporter System (Promega, USA) following the manufacturer’s manual. Each assay was carried out in triplicate, with data from one representative experiment shown.

Fish feeding and sample collection: Groupers (Epinephelus lanceolatus, 5 g) were obtained from Laizhou Mingbo Aquatic Products Co., Ltd. (China), and reared in a circulating water system under 26±2°C, 25‰–30‰ salinity, and 6–7 mg/L dissolved oxygen conditions. Fish were fed commercial diet once daily for two weeks before use. A total of 240 healthy fish were then selected and randomly divided into a MOCK group and SGIV infection group (four replicates each group), injected with 100 μL of phosphatebuffered saline (PBS) and SGIV (105.5 TCID50/mL), respectively. At 1, 3, 5, and 7 days post-infection (dpi), three fish from each group were euthanized by methanesulfonate (MS222, 250 mg/mL), and their livers were immediately collected and stored at −80°C for determination of viral replication dynamics. At 3 and 5 dpi, liver samples from six fish in the MOCK and SGIV groups were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for histopathological analysis. To investigate the molecular events occurring in the livers of the infected fish, 12 fish from both the control and infection groups were euthanized at 5 dpi, followed by the immediate collection of blood and liver. Blood was collected and stored at 4°C for serum separation. Livers were stored at −80°C or in liquid nitrogen for biochemical, metabolomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic analyses. In a parallel set of experiments, 30 fish from both groups were used to calculate the survival rates of groupers upon SGIV infection. All animal protocols were approved by the Ethics Committee of Experimental Animals, South China Agricultural University (approval No.2020g009, 2020.9.8).

evidence.original_text: Histological analysis indicated that SGIV-infected livers exhibited a significant decrease in hepatic lipid content, characterized by fewer lipid droplets and a reduced oil red O staining area in an infection-time dependent manner. In contrast, hepatic lipid content in the MOCK group did not change significantly between 3 and 5 dpi (Figure 1A, B). Accordingly, levels of TG and TC in the liver and serum of SGIV-infected groupers were decreased compared to the mock group (Figure 1C, D). The profiles of three lipoproteins were also detected, with LDL levels in the liver and serum and HDL and VLDL levels in the serum significantly decreased upon SGIV infection. These findings suggest that lipid homeostasis in the liver is disturbed during SGIV infection.

entries.context: SGIV-infected grouper liver at 3 and 5 dpi compared to mock group, oil red O staining

evidence.original_text: The volcano plots of DEMs, DEGs, and DEPs between the SGIV-infected and MOCK groups (Supplementary Figure S2C) revealed 93 up-regulated and 129 down-regulated DEMs, 5 931 up-regulated and 6 899 down-regulated DEGs, and 174 up-regulated and 283 down-regulated DEPs in the SGIV-infected group compared to the MOCK group. Most DEMs were grouped into organic acids and derivatives (26.42%), lipids and lipid-like molecules (25.94%), organic oxygen compounds (17.92%), nucleosides, nucleotides, and analogs (11.32%), and organoheterocyclic compounds (7.55%) (Figure 2A; Supplementary Figure S3).

entries.context: SGIV-infected vs MOCK grouper liver, metabolomic analysis

evidence.original_text: Results showed that an intraperitoneal injection of 100 μL of SGIV (10^5.5 TCID50/mL) resulted in a mortality rate of approximately 80% compared to the mock group (Supplementary Figure S1B). Moreover, mRNA expression levels of viral core genes, including MCP and VP19, increased significantly in the liver with infection time (Supplementary Figure S1C), indicating effective SGIV replication in the liver in a time-dependent manner.

entries.context: Groupers intraperitoneally injected with SGIV (10^5.5 TCID50/mL)

evidence.original_text: ALA treatment not only inhibited the mRNA levels of SGIV genes, including MCP, VP19, and VP118 (Figure 6C, D, F), but also decreased the protein synthesis of SGIV MCP, VP19, and VP118 in a dose-dependent manner (Figure 6E). In addition, the titer assay indicated that ALA treatment significantly inhibited SGIV progeny virus in GS cells (Figure 6G). Thus, these findings suggest that ALA exerts antiviral activity upon SGIV infection in vitro. To gain insights into the potential mechanisms of the antiviral effects of ALA, we evaluated whether the antioxidant pathway was involved in the antiviral actions of ALA. Nrf2 is a major regulator of the transcription of antioxidant-related genes. The Nrf2/ARE signaling pathway is the most important endogenous antioxidant pathway and is expected to significantly impact the prevention and treatment of virus-induced oxidative injury (Wang et al., 2022). As shown in Figure 6H, although exogenous supplementation of ALA did not significantly affect ARE activity compared to the mock group, it notably restored the decreased ARE promoter activity caused by SGIV infection. In addition, compared to the mock group, the mRNA and protein expression levels of Nrf2 were significantly increased in the ALA-supplemented group. SGIV infection significantly reduced Nrf2 gene and protein expression levels, but these inhibitory effects were significantly rescued by ALA treatment (Figure 6I, J). Thus, we propose that the antioxidant properties of ALA are essential for its antiviral activity against SGIV infection.

Compared to the control cells, the addition of GA markedly exacerbated the severity of the CPEs induced by SGIV infection at 24 hpi (Figure 4B). At the transcriptional level, GA significantly increased the levels of SGIV MCP, VP19, and VP118 in a dose-dependent manner (Figure 4C, D, F). Consistently, western blotting showed that the expression levels of viral proteins MCP, VP19, and VP118 increased in a dose-dependent manner after pretreatment with different doses of GA (Figure 4E). Moreover, the addition of 4 mmol/L GA significantly increased SGIV production (Figure 4G). Thus, these results indicate that exogenous supplementation of GA can promote SGIV proliferation.

Although 20 μmol/L LA had no obvious inhibitory effects, 40 μmol/L LA markedly delayed CPE progression induced by SGIV infection (Figure 5B). At the transcriptional level, 40 μmol/L LA exerted significant inhibitory effects on SGIV MCP, VP19, and VP118 (Figure 5C, D, F). Consistently, 40 μmol/L LA significantly inhibited the protein synthesis of MCP, VP19, and VP118 (Figure 5E), as well as the production of SGIV during virus infection (Figure 5G). Thus, these observations indicate that LA exerts antiviral activity against SGIV infection.

Iridovirus poses a substantial threat to global aquaculture due to its high mortality rate; however, the molecular mechanisms underpinning its pathogenesis are not well elucidated. Here, a multi-omics approach was applied to groupers infected with Singapore grouper iridovirus (SGIV), focusing on the roles of key metabolites. Results showed that SGIV induced obvious histopathological damage and changes in metabolic enzymes within the liver. Furthermore, SGIV significantly reduced the contents of lipid droplets, triglycerides, cholesterol, and lipoproteins. Metabolomic analysis indicated that the altered metabolites were enriched in 19 pathways, with a notable down-regulation of lipid metabolites such as glycerophosphates and alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), consistent with disturbed lipid homeostasis in the liver. Integration of transcriptomic and metabolomic data revealed that the top enriched pathways were related to cell growth and death and nucleotide, carbohydrate, amino acid, and lipid metabolism, supporting the conclusion that SGIV infection induced liver metabolic reprogramming. Further integrative transcriptomic and proteomic analysis indicated that SGIV infection activated crucial molecular events in a phagosome-immune depression-metabolism dysregulation-necrosis signaling cascade. Of note, integrative multi-omics analysis demonstrated the consumption of ALA and linoleic acid (LA) metabolites, and the accumulation of L-glutamic acid (GA), accompanied by alterations in immune, inflammation, and cell death-related genes. Further experimental data showed that ALA, but not GA, suppressed SGIV replication by activating antioxidant and anti-inflammatory responses in the host. Collectively, these findings provide a comprehensive resource for understanding host response dynamics during fish iridovirus infection and highlight the antiviral potential of ALA in the prevention and treatment of iridoviral diseases.

In summary, this study examined the pathogenic changes and molecular events in the livers of groupers following SGIV infection. SGIV infection resulted in liver damage and lipid homeostasis disruption. Multi-omics analysis revealed that SGIV infection not only induced metabolic reprogramming but also activated crucial molecular events in a phagosome-immune depression-metabolism dysregulation-necrosis signaling cascade in the liver. Moreover, LA metabolism and arginine biosynthesis were identified as key target pathways involved in SGIV infection. Exogenous supplementation of ALA demonstrated antiviral effects against SGIV replication by regulating host antioxidant and pro-inflammatory responses. Thus, our findings systematically elucidated molecular events, such as metabolic reprogramming, in the liver in SGIV-infected grouper and identified the antiviral functions of key metabolites. Collectively, these insights not only deepen our understanding of the mechanisms underlying iridovirus pathogenesis, but also propose novel candidates for the prevention and control of iridoviral diseases in aquaculture.

However, whether supplementation with ALA exerts antiviral activity against SGIV infection in groupers requires further investigation. The detailed roles of these core DEMs and DEGs/DEPs in SGIV-induced inflammation, metabolic reprogramming, and necrosis require further investigation. The liver, a central metabolic and immunological organ in vertebrates ranging from fish to primates, plays a critical role in the capture and clearance of pathogenic microorganisms. Liver damage, often accompanied by changes in liver enzyme levels, typically occurs upon pathogen attack. Not only do hepatotropic viruses, such as hepatitis A, B, C, and E, replicate well in the liver, making it their primary target, but other viruses, including SARS-CoV-2, also induce liver damage and dysfunction in severe and critical patients. During infections, human parainfluenza virus 3 (HPIV-3) can lead to an elevation in liver enzyme levels, including AST, ALT, and LDH. Tilapia Lake virus disease (TiLVD) can result in severe necrotic hepatitis in tilapia. In SGIV-infected grouper, hybridization signals of SGIV can be detected in epithelial, endothelial, and subendothelial hypertrophic cells of different tissues, including the spleen, kidney, and liver. Our results further confirmed that SGIV infection evoked gradual histopathological changes with infection time, as evidenced by the increase in cell membrane rupture and hepatocyte atrophy, vacuolization, and necrosis. Moreover, the elevation in liver metabolic enzymes, including ALT, AST, ALP, ACP, and LDH, was consistent with the presence of severe liver damage and necrosis after SGIV infection. Therefore, severe pathological changes in the liver may be an important factor contributing to the high mortality associated with SGIV disease in grouper aquaculture.

However, whether supplementation with ALA exerts antiviral activity against SGIV infection in groupers requires further investigation. The detailed roles of these core DEMs and DEGs/DEPs in SGIV-induced inflammation, metabolic reprogramming, and necrosis require further investigation. Accumulating evidence has demonstrated that metabolites can exert proviral or antiviral effects in different stages of infection, including viral entry, replication, and release. For instance, LA can bind to PEDV NSP5 and attenuate AKT phosphorylation of the PI3K pathway to inhibit PEDV replication and release. ALA can interrupt the binding, adsorption, and entry stages of Zika virus (ZIKV) replication. To determine whether the key metabolites identified in our study showed different activities in SGIV pathogenesis, we evaluated the effects of several metabolites on SGIV replication in vitro. Interestingly, exogenous supplementation of GA significantly promoted SGIV replication, while the addition of LA and ALA significantly inhibited SGIV replication. ALA is reported to exhibit antioxidant activities by up-regulating Nrf2 and its downstream target genes and exert anti-inflammation effects by down-regulating the mRNA levels of pro-inflammatory factors. Thus, we further evaluated whether ALA regulated ARE/Nrf2 and NF-κB signaling during SGIV infection. Of note, our results showed that the addition of exogenous ALA significantly increased ARE promoter activity and up-regulated Nrf2 expression, but also decreased NF-κB promoter activity and down-regulated the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, including TNFα and IL1β, upon SGIV infection. These findings suggest that the antiviral activity of ALA is likely due to its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects in response to SGIV proliferation.
